---
title: "Coordinate reference systems"
subtitle: "Why spatial data is special"
author: "Jannes Muenchow, Jakub Nowosad"
date: 2026-07-06
---

## Coordinate reference systems

Coordinates are just numbers. A **CRS** ties them to a place on Earth.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Geographic** CRS:<br>London = `c(-0.1, 51.5)` (degrees lon/lat)

![](/img/vector_lonlat.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Projected** CRS:<br>London = `c(530000, 180000)` (metres E/N, British National Grid)

![](/img/vector_projected.png)
:::
:::

## Geographic vs projected

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
::: {.incremental}
- **Geographic CRS** — longitude/latitude in **degrees** on a datum + ellipsoid.
  Example: **WGS 84**, EPSG:4326. Global, but a degree is not a constant distance.
- **Projected CRS** — **x/y in metres** on a flat plane. Example: **UTM**, LAEA,
  National Grid. Needed for distances, areas and many geometry operations.
- Flattening the round Earth **always distorts** something. Every projection is a
  compromise between area, distance, shape and direction.

CRSs are identified by **EPSG codes** and stored as **WKT2**.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
![](/img/goode_homolosine.jpg){fig-alt="World map in Goode's homolosine projection, resembling a peeled orange laid flat"}
:::
:::

## Which CRS when?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Store / share / GPS / web maps** → **EPSG:4326 (WGS 84)**
- **True areas** → an **equal-area** projection, e.g. **LAEA** (EPSG:3035) for Europe
- **True distances / local analysis** → a suitable **UTM** zone (metres)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](/img/tissot_mercator.svg){width=70% fig-alt="Mercator projection with Tissot indicatrices showing area distortion"}

![](/img/tissot_mollweide.svg){width=70% fig-alt="Mollweide equal-area projection with uniform Tissot indicatrices"}

*Tissot's indicatrices. Eric Gaba, [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)*
:::
:::

## CRS in practice

::: {.incremental}
- Datasets must **share a CRS** before you combine them.
- Choose an appropriate **projected** CRS when measuring distances or areas.
- In R: `st_crs()` / `st_transform()` (**sf**), `crs()` / `project()` (**terra**).
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
## CRS ≠ the math engine
Your CRS *labels* coordinates; the **geometry engine** decides the computation.
Project to metres to use fast **Cartesian** math; on lon/lat, spherical or ellipsoidal math is used instead.
:::

## Sidenote: geometry on a sphere (S2)

::: {.incremental}
- The old way treated lon/lat as if it were a **flat plane** — giving wrong distances, areas and intersections unless you projected first.
- Since **sf 1.0**, sf uses Google's **S2** engine: geometry is computed on a **sphere**, so lon/lat operations are correct *without* projecting.
- A sphere is still an approximation of Earth, but far better than pretending degrees are Cartesian.
:::

. . .

Switch back to planar GEOS with `sf_use_s2(FALSE)`.

## CRS exercise

Use the `nz` dataset from **spData**.

1. Find its CRS with `st_crs(nz)`.
2. Is it **geographic** or **projected**?
3. Reproject it to **EPSG:4326** and plot the result.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Hint
If the coordinate values are in metres, the CRS is probably projected.
:::

Solution script: [`solutions/02-crs-solution.R`](../solutions/02-crs-solution.R)
